Chapter 10: The Shapes of Molecules
10.1 Depicting Molecules and Ions with Lewis Structures.
10.2 Valence-Shell Electron-Pair Repulsion (V S E P R) Theory.
10.3 Molecular Shape and Molecular Polarity.

Steps In Converting a Molecular Formula Into a Lewis Structure
Figure 10.1

Example: NF 3
Step 1: N has a lower E N than F, so N is placed in the center.
Step 2: N has five valence electrons, and each F has seven:
Step 3: Draw a single bond from each surrounding atom to the central atom; this uses 6 e ⁻ , leaving    26 – 6 = 20 e ⁻
Step 4: Distribute the remaining electrons in pairs so that each atom ends up with 8e ⁻ (or 2e ⁻ for H). This uses the remaining 20 e ⁻ .

Sample Problem 10.1: Problem
Writing Lewis Structures for Species with Single Bonds and One Central Atom
PROBLEM: Write a Lewis structure for the following:
(a) CCl 2 F 2 (b)

Sample Problem 10.1: Solution (a)
SOLUTION (a): CCl 2 F 2
Step 1 : Place the atoms relative to each other. Carbon has the lowest EN and is the central atom.  The other atoms are placed around it.
Step 2 :
Step 3 : Draw single bonds to the central atom and subtract 2 e ⁻ for each bond: 32 – 8 = 24 e ⁻
Step 4 : Use the remaining 24 e ⁻ to give each atom a full octet.

Sample Problem 10.1: Solution (b)
SOLUTION (b):
Step 1 : Place the atoms relative to each other. Carbon has the lowest E N and is the central atom. The other atoms are placed around it.
Step 2 :
Step 3 : Draw single bonds to the central atom and subtract 2 e ⁻ for each bond: 20 – 4 = 16 e ⁻
Step 4 : Use the remaining 16 e ⁻ to give each atom a full octet.
Since this species is an ion, place brackets around the Lewis structure and add the 1− charge as a superscript.

Sample Problem 10.2: Problem
Writing Lewis Structures for Molecules with Single Bonds and More Than One Central Atom
PROBLEM: Write the Lewis structure for methanol (molecular formula CH 4 O), an important industrial alcohol that can be used as a gasoline alternative in cars.

Sample Problem 10.2: Solution
SOLUTION:
Step 1 : Place the atoms relative to each other. H can only form one bond, so C and O must be central and adjacent to each other.
Step 2 :
Step 3 : Draw single bonds and subtract 2 e ⁻ for each bond: 14 – 10 = 4 e ⁻
Step 4 : Use the remaining 4 e ⁻ to give each atom a full octet (H needs only 2 e ⁻ ).

Multiple Bonds
If there are not enough electrons for the central atom to attain an octet, a multiple bond is needed.
Step 5 :  If the central atom does not have a full octet, change a lone pair on a surrounding atom into another bonding pair to the central atom, thus forming a multiple bond.

Sample Problem 10.3: Problem
Writing Lewis Structures for Molecules with Multiple Bonds
PROBLEM: Write Lewis structures for the following:
(a) Ethylene (C 2 H 4 ), the most important reactant in the manufacture of polymers
(b) Nitrogen (N 2 ), the most abundant atmospheric gas

Sample Problem 10.3: Solution (a)
SOLUTION (a):
Step 1 : Place the atoms relative to each other. In C 2 H 4 ,the two C atoms must be bonded to each other since H atoms can have only one bond .
Step 2 : Determine the total number of valence electrons (C is in Group 14; H is in Group 1):
Step 3 : Add single bonds and subtract 2e ⁻ for each bond:
5 bonds × 2e ⁻ = 10e ⁻ so 12e ⁻ − 10e ⁻ = 2e ⁻ remaining.
Step 4 : Distribute the remaining valence electrons to attain octets .
Step 5 : Change a lone pair to a bonding pair. The right C has an octet, but the left C has only 6e ⁻ , so we change the lone pair on the right C to another bonding pair between the two C atoms .

Sample Problem 10.3: Solution (b)
SOLUTION (a):
Step 1 : Place the atoms relative to each other.
Step 2 : Determine the total number of valence electrons (N is in Group 15):
Step 3 : Add single bonds and subtract 2e ⁻ for each bond:
1 bonds × 2e ⁻ = 2e ⁻ so 10e ⁻ − 2e ⁻ = 8e ⁻ remaining.
Step 4 : Distribute the remaining valence electrons to attain octets.
Step 5 : The N on the left ends up with an octet, but the N on the right does not, so we change a lone pair to a bonding pair. In this case, moving one lone pair to make a double bond still does not give both N atoms an octet, so we also move a second lone pair make a triple bond.
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide74.xml]

Resonance Structures: Lewis Structures
O 3 can be drawn in two ways:
These are two different reasonable Lewis structures for the same molecule.
Neither structure depicts O 3 accurately, because in reality the
bonds are identical in length and energy:
A
single bond has a bond length of 148 pm.
A
double bond has a bond length of 121 pm.
The bonds in O 3 are identical with a bond length of 128 pm.

Resonance Structures: Arrows
The structure of O 3 is shown more correctly using both Lewis structures, called resonance structures .
A two-headed resonance arrow is placed between them.
Resonance structures have the same relative placement of atoms but different locations of bonding and lone electron pairs.
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide75.xml]

The Resonance Hybrid
A species like O 3 , which can be depicted by more than one valid Lewis structure, is best represented as a resonance hybrid.
Resonance forms are not real bonding depictions.
O 3 does not change back and forth between its two resonance forms.
The real structure of the resonance hybrid for O 3 is an average of its contributing resonance forms.

A Purple Mule
A mule is a genetic mix, a hybrid, of a horse and a donkey. It is not a horse one instant and a donkey the next. Likewise, a resonance hybrid has a single structure although it retains characteristics of its resonance forms.

Electron Delocalization
Lewis structures depict electrons as localized either on an individual atom (lone pairs) or in a bond between two atoms (shared pair).
In a resonance hybrid, electrons are delocalized: their density is “spread” over a few adjacent atoms.
Dotted lines are used to show delocalized electrons.

Fractional Bond Orders
Resonance structures often have fractional bond orders due to partial bonding.

Formal Charge
Formal charge is the charge an atom would have if all electrons were shared equally .
Formal charge of atom =
For
in resonance form I, the formal charge is given by
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide76.xml]

Formal Charge: All Resonance Forms
Formal charges must sum to the actual charge on the species for all resonance forms.
For both these resonance forms the formal charges sum to zero, since O 3 is a neutral molecule.
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide77.xml]

Choosing the More Important Resonance Form
Smaller formal charges (positive or negative) are preferable to larger ones.
The same nonzero formal charges on adjacent atoms are not preferred.
Avoid like charges on adjacent atoms.
A more negative formal charge should reside on a more electronegative atom.

Preferred Resonance Form of NCO ⁻
Resonance forms with smaller formal charges are preferred. Resonance form
is therefore not an important contributor.
A negative formal charge should be placed on a more electronegative atom, so resonance form III is preferred to resonance form II since O is more electronegative than N.
The overall structure of the NCO ⁻ ion is a weighted average of all three forms; resonance forms II and III contribute more than
, and
III contributes more than II.

Formal Charge Versus Oxidation Number
For a formal charge , bonding electrons are shared equally by the atoms.
The formal charge of an atom may change between resonance forms.
For an oxidation number , bonding electrons are transferred to the more electronegative atom.
The oxidation number of an atom is the same in all resonance forms.

Sample Problem 10.4: Problem and Plan
Writing Resonance Structures and Assigning Formal Charges
PROBLEM: Write resonance structures for N 2 O (one N is the central atom)​​, assign formal charges to the atoms in each resonance structure, and find the bond order. Use formal charges to determine which resonance structure is most important.
PLAN: We write a Lewis structure, moving lone and bonding pairs to write other resonance structures and connect them with resonance arrows. We assign formal charges. The bond order is the number of shared electron pairs divided by the number of atom pairs. To determine the most important resonance structure, look for low formal charges, with the more negative formal charge on the more electronegative atom.

Sample Problem 10.4: Solution 1
SOLUTION:
Steps 1 and 2. One N is in the center, so the other N and the O are each bonded to the central N. There are
valence e ⁻ .
Steps 3 and 4.
Step 5: Since the central N has only 4e ⁻ , it needs two more bonds.

Sample Problem 10.4: Solution 2
Formal charge of atom =
Structure A:
Outside N:
Central N:
O:
Structure C:
Outside N:
Central N:

Structure B:
Outside N:

The bond order is
Structure C has the highest formal charges, so it is not an important contributor to the resonance hybrid. A and B have the same magnitude of formal charges, but B has the negative charge on O, the more electronegative atom, so structure B is the most important contributor.

Exceptions to the Octet Rule
Molecules with Electron-Deficient Atoms
B and Be are commonly electron-deficient.
Molecules with Odd-Electron Atoms
A few molecules have an odd number of valence electrons; they are called free radicals, species that contain a lone (unpaired) electron.
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide79.xml]

Exceptions to the Octet Rule: Expanded Valence Shells
Expanded Valence Shells: more than eight electrons around the central atom.
An expanded valence shell is only possible for nonmetals from Period 3 or higher because these elements have available d orbitals .

Examples of Expanded Valence Shells
The central atom is bonded to more than four atoms.
The central atom is bonded to four or fewer atoms.

Expanded Valence Shells and Formal Charge
The concept of formal charge can be applied to draw Lewis structures for species in which an atom has an expanded valence shell.
One example: A Lewis structure can be drawn for H 2 SO 4 that obeys the octet rule, but it has several nonzero formal charges. A Lewis structure in which sulfur has an expanded octet can be drawn; in this Lewis structure, all atoms have zero formal charges.

Sample Problem 10.5: Problem and Plan
Writing Lewis Structures for Octet-Rule Exceptions
PROBLEM: Write a Lewis structure and identify the octet-rule exception for,
(a) XeF 4
(b) H 3 P O 4 (draw two resonance structures and select the more important)
(c) BFCl 2
PLAN: We write each Lewis structure and examine it for exceptions to the octet rule.
(a) and (b) The central atoms are in Periods 5 and 3, respectively, so they can have more than an octet.
(c) The central atom is B, which can have fewer than an octet of   electrons.

Sample Problem 10.5: Solution (a)
SOLUTION (a):
XeF 4 has a total of 36 valence electrons: 8 form the bonds and 24 complete the octets of the F atoms. The remaining 4 valence electrons are placed on the central atom, resulting in an expanded valence shell:

Sample Problem 10.5: Solution (b)
SOLUTION (b):
H 3 P O 4 has 32 valence electrons: 14 form bonds and 18 complete the octets of the O and P atoms (
shown with formal charges). Another
resonance structure (structure II) can be drawn in which a lone pair from the O atom with a nonzero formal charge is changed to a bonding pair.
obeys the octet rule but has nonzero formal charges. Structure II
has an expanded valence shell with zero formal charges. According to formal charge rules, structure II is the more important structure.

Sample Problem 10.5: Solution (c)
SOLUTION (c):
BFCl 2 has 24 valence electrons: 6 form bonds and 18 complete the octets of the F and Cl atoms. This molecule has an electron-deficient atom (incomplete octet); B has only six electrons surrounding it:

Valence-Shell Electron-Pair Repulsion Theory (V S E P R)
Each group of valence electrons around a central atom is located as far as possible from the others, to minimize repulsions.
A “group” of electrons is any number of electrons that occupies a localized region around an atom.
A single bond, double bond, triple bond, lone pair, or single electron all count as a single group.
Only electron groups around the central atom affect shape; electrons on atoms other than the central atom do not.
The molecular shape is the three-dimensional arrangement of nuclei joined by the bonding groups.

Electron-group Repulsions and Molecular Shapes
Figure 10.2
Source: (A) Stephen Frisch/McGraw Hill

Electron-group Arrangement vs Molecular Shape
The electron-group arrangement is defined by both bonding and nonbonding electron groups.
The molecular shape is the three-dimensional arrangement of nuclei joined by the bonding groups. This is defined only by the relative positions of the nuclei.
Molecular shape is classified using the following designation:
A = central atom
X = surrounding atom
E = nonbonding valence electron group
m and n are integers

Bond Angle
The bond angle is the angle formed by the nuclei of two surrounding atoms with the nucleus of the central atom.
The angles shown in Figure 10.2 are ideal bond angles, determined by basic geometry alone. Real bond angles deviate from the ideal value in many cases.

Two Electron Groups: Linear Electron-group Arrangement
Figure 10.3

Three Electron Groups: Trigonal Planar Electron-group Arrangement
Figure 10.4

Factors Affecting Bond Angles
Nonbonding (Lone) Pairs.
A lone pair repels bonding pairs more strongly than bonding pairs repel each other. This decreases the angle between the bonding pairs.
Double Bonds.
A double bond has greater electron density than a single bond, and repels the single bond electrons more than they repel each other.
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide80.xml]

Four Electron Groups: Tetrahedral Electron-group Arrangement
Figure 10.5
Source: Stephen Frisch/McGraw Hill

Lewis Structures Do Not Indicate Molecular Shape
Figure 10.6

Five Electron Groups: Trigonal Bipyramidal Electron-group Arrangement
Figure 10.7

Axial and Equatorial Positions
A five electron-group system has two different positions for electron groups, and two ideal bond angles.
Where possible, lone pairs in a five electron-group system occupy equatorial positions.
Equatorial-equatorial repulsions are weaker than axial-equatorial repulsions.

Six Electron Groups: Octahedral Electron-group Arrangement
Figure 10.8

A Summary of Common Molecular Shapes with Two to Four Electron Groups
Figure 10.9

A Summary of Common Molecular Shapes with Five or Six Electron Groups
Figure 10.9

The Four Steps in Converting a Molecular Formula to a Molecular Shape
Figure 10.10

Sample Problem 10.6: Problem
Examining Shapes with Two, Three, or Four Electron Groups
PROBLEM: Draw the molecular shapes and predict the bond angles (relative to the ideal angles) of:
(a) PF 3 and (b) C O Cl 2

Sample Problem 10.6: Solution (a)
SOLUTION (a): For PF 3 :
Step 1. Write the Lewis structure from the formula (see below).
Step 2. Assign the electron-group arrangement: Three bonding groups and one lone pair give four electron groups around P and the tetrahedral arrangement.
Step 3. Predict the bond angle: The ideal bond angle in a tetrahedron is 109.5°. There is one lone pair, so the actual bond angle will be less than 109.5°.
Step 4. Draw and name the molecular shape: With one lone pair, PF 3 has a trigonal pyramidal shape (A X 3 E):

Sample Problem 10.6: Solution (b)
SOLUTION (b): For C OCl 2 .
Step 1. Write the Lewis structure from the formula (see below).
Step 2. Assign the electron-group arrangement: Two single bonds and one double bond give three electron groups around C and the trigonal planar arrangement.
Step 3. Predict the bond angles: The ideal bond angle in the trigonal planar arrangement is 120°, but the double bond between C and O will compress       the
angle to less than 120°.
Step 4. Draw and name the molecular shape: With three electron groups and no lone pairs, C OCl 2 has a trigonal planar shape (AX 3 )
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide81.xml]

Sample Problem 10.7: Problem, Plan, and Solution (a) 1
Examining Shapes with Five or Six Electron Groups
PROBLEM: Draw the molecular shapes and predict the bond angles (relative to the ideal angles) of
(a) SbF 5 and (b) BrF 5
PLAN: We proceed as in Sample Problem 10.6, being sure to minimize the number of axial-equatorial repulsions.
SOLUTION (a): For SbF 5 .
Step 1. Lewis structure (see next slide).
Step 2. Electron-group arrangement: With five electron groups (five bonding pairs), this molecule has the trigonal bipyramidal arrangement.

Sample Problem 10.7: Problem, Plan, and Solution (a) 2
Step 3. Bond angles: All the groups and surrounding atoms are identical, so the bond angles are ideal: 120° between equatorial groups and 90° between axial and equatorial groups.
Step 4. Molecular shape: Five electron groups and no lone pairs give the trigonal bipyramidal shape (AX 5 ).

Sample Problem 10.7: Solution (b)
SOLUTION (b): For BrF 5 .
Step 1. Lewis structure (see below).
Step 2. Electron-group arrangement: Six electron groups (five bonding pairs and one lone pair) give the octahedral arrangement.
Step 3. Bond angles: The lone pair will make all bond angles less than the ideal 90°.
Step 4. Molecular shape: With one lone pair, BrF 5 has the square pyramidal shape (AX 5 E).

Molecular Shapes with More Than One Central Atom
The shapes of molecules with more than one central atom are composites of the shapes around each of the atom.
Ethane (CH 3 CH 3 ): With four bonding groups and no lone pairs around the two central carbons, ethane is shaped like two overlapping tetrahedra.
Ethanol (CH 3 CH 2 OH): The
groups have
four bonding groups and are tetrahedrally shaped; the O atom has two bonding groups and two lone pairs around it, so the
group has a bent shape.

The Overall Shapes of Ethane and Ethanol
Figure 10.11

Sample Problem 10.8: Problem and Plan
Predicting Molecular Shapes with More Than One Central Atom
PROBLEM: Determine the shape around each central atom in acetone,
PLAN: There are three central C atoms, two of which are in
groups. We determine the shape around one central
atom at a time.

Sample Problem 10.8: Solution
SOLUTION:
Step 1. Lewis structure (see below).
Step 2. Electron-group arrangement: Each
group has four electron groups
around its central C, so its electron-group arrangement is tetrahedral . The third C atom has three electron groups around it, so it has the trigonal planar arrangement.
Step 3. Bond angles: The
angle in
should be near the
ideal 109.5°. The
double bond will compress the
angle to less than
the ideal 120°.
Step 4. Shapes around central atoms: With four electron groups and no lone pairs, the shape around C in each
is tetrahedral (AX 4 ). With three electron
groups and no lone pairs, the shape around the middle C is trigonal planar (AX 3 ):

Molecular Shape and Molecular Polarity
Overall molecular polarity depends on both shape and bond polarity.
The polarity of a molecule is measured by its dipole moment ( μ ) which is given in the unit debye (D).
A molecule is polar if:
it contains one or more polar bonds and
the individual bond dipoles do not cancel.

The Orientation of Polar Molecules in an Electric Field
Figure 10.12

Bond Polarity, Bond Angle, and Dipole Moment: C O 2
Example: C O 2 .
The ΔEN between C (E N = 2.5) and O (E N = 3.5) makes each
bond polar.
C O 2 is linear, the bond angle is 180°, and the individual bond polarities therefore cancel. The molecule has no net dipole moment ( μ = 0 D).
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide82.xml]

Bond Polarity, Bond Angle, and Dipole Moment: H 2 O
Example: H 2 O
The ΔEN between H (E N = 2.1) and O (E N = 3.5) makes each
bond polar.
H 2 O has a bent geometry and the individual bond polarities do not cancel. This molecule has an overall molecular polarity . The O is partially negative while the H atoms are partially positive.
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide83.xml]

Bond Polarity, Bond Angle, and Dipole Moment: Same Shapes, Different Polarities
Molecules with the same shape may have different polarities.
Bonds are polar, but individual bond polarities cancel in CCl 4 .
Bond polarities do not cancel in CHCl 3 . This molecule is polar   ( μ = 1.01 D).

Sample Problem 10.9: Problem and Plan
Predicting the Polarity of Molecules
PROBLEM: For each of the following, use the molecular shape and E N values and trends to predict the direction of bond and molecular polarity, if present:
(a) ammonia, NH 3
(b) boron trifluoride, BF 3
(c) carbonyl sulfide, C O S (atom sequence S C O).
PLAN: We draw and name the molecular shape and point a polar arrow toward the atom with higher E N in each bond. If the bond polarities balance one another, the molecule is nonpolar; if they reinforce each other, we show the direction of the molecular polarity.

Sample Problem 10.9: Solution (a)
SOLUTION (a): For NH 3 . The molecular shape is trigonal pyramidal. N (E N = 3.0) is more electronegative than H (E N = 2.1), so the bond polarities point toward N and partially reinforce each other; thus the molecular polarity points toward N.
Therefore, ammonia is polar.

Sample Problem 10.9: Solution (b)
SOLUTION (b): For BF 3 . The molecular shape is trigonal planar. F (E N = 4.0) is farther to the right in Period 2 than B (E N = 2.0), so it is more electronegative; thus, each bond polarity points toward F. However, the bond angle is 120°, so the three bond polarities balance each other, and BF 3 has no molecular polarity.
Therefore, boron trifluoride is nonpolar.

Sample Problem 10.9: Solution (c)
SOLUTION (c): For COS. The molecular shape is linear. With C and S having the same E N, the
bond is nonpolar, but the
bond is quite
polar (Δ E N = 1.0), so there is a net molecular polarity toward the O.
Therefore, carbonyl sulfide is polar.

The Effect of Molecular Polarity on Behavior
Example: The cis and trans isomers of C 2 H 2 Cl 2 .
The cis isomer is polar while the trans isomer is not. The boiling point of the cis isomer is 13 ° C higher than that of the trans isomer.

Chemical Connections: Molecular Shape and Biological Receptors
Figure B10.1

Resonance Structures: Arrows - Text Alternative

Structure 1 shows central oxygen atom B bonded to oxygen atom A by a double bond and oxygen atom C by a single bond. Oxygen atom A carries two lone pairs of electrons, oxygen atom B carries one lone pair of electrons, and oxygen atom C carries three lone pairs of electrons. A lone pair of electrons from oxygen atom C moves to the bond between oxygen atoms C and B. A bond pair of electrons from the double bond between oxygen atoms B and A moves to the oxygen atom A. Structure 2 shows central oxygen atom B bonded to oxygen atom A by a single bond and oxygen atom C by a double bond. Oxygen atom A carries three lone pairs of electrons, oxygen atom B carries one lone pair of electrons, and oxygen atom C carries two lone pairs of electrons. A lone pair of electrons from oxygen atom A moves to the bond between oxygen atoms A and B. A bond pair of electrons from the double bond between oxygen atoms B and C moves to the oxygen atom C.

Sample Problem 10.4: Solution 1 - Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide26.xml]
Top image: A central nitrogen atom is surrounded by a nitrogen atom and an oxygen atom. Step 3 leads to the formation of the following structure: a central nitrogen atom is bonded to a nitrogen atom and an oxygen atom by single bonds. Accompanying calculation reads, two bonds times two electrons equals four electrons; 16 electrons minus four electrons equals 12 electrons. Steps 4 leads to the formation of the following Lewis structure: a central nitrogen atom is bonded to a nitrogen atom and an oxygen atom by single bonds. The nitrogen atom on the end and the oxygen atom carry three lone pairs of electrons. In the Lewis structure, 12 electrons are distributed.
Bottom image: Structure A shows a central nitrogen atom bonded to a nitrogen atom and an oxygen atom by double bonds. The nitrogen atom on the end carries two lone pairs of electrons and minus 1 charge, the nitrogen atom in the center carries plus 1 charge, and the oxygen atom carries two lone pairs of electrons and zero charge. Structure B shows a central nitrogen atom bonded to a nitrogen atom by a triple bond and an oxygen atom by a single bond. The nitrogen atom on the end carries a lone pair of electrons and zero charge, the nitrogen atom in the center carries plus 1 charge, and the oxygen atom carries three lone pairs of electrons and minus 1 charge. Structure C shows a central nitrogen atom bonded to a nitrogen atom by a single bond and an oxygen atom by a triple bond. The nitrogen atom on the end carries three lone pairs of electrons and minus 2 charge, the nitrogen atom in the center carries plus 1 charge, and the oxygen atom carries one lone pair of electrons and plus 1 charge.
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide26.xml]

Exceptions to the Octet Rule - Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide28.xml]
Top image: Lewis structure of beryllium chloride shows a central beryllium atom bonded to two chlorine atoms. Each chlorine atom carries three lone pairs of electrons and zero charge. Beryllium atom carries zero charge. Lewis structure of boron trifluoride shows a central boron atom bonded to three fluorine atoms. Each fluorine atom carries three lone pairs of electrons and zero charge. Boron atom carries zero charge.
Bottom image: The first structure shows a central nitrogen atom carrying a lone electron and plus 1 charge bonded to an oxygen atom carrying three lone pairs of electrons and minus 1 charge by a single bond and another oxygen atom carrying two lone pairs of electrons and zero charge by a double bond. The second structure shows a central nitrogen atom carrying a lone pair of electrons and zero charge bonded to an oxygen atom carrying a lone electron and two lone pairs of electrons, and zero charge by a single bond and another oxygen atom carrying two lone pairs of electrons and zero charge by a double bond.
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide28.xml]

Factors Affecting Bond Angles - Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide42.xml]
The structure 1 has a central sulfur atom is bonded to 2 oxygen atoms by double bonds. The sulfur atom carries a lone pair of electrons, and each oxygen atom carries two lone pairs of electrons. Angle between the two double bonds is 119 degrees. The structure 2 is a ball-and-stick model shows a central yellow sphere, and an yellow colored orbital with two electrons above it, is bonded to 2 red spheres by double bonds. The structure 3 is an actual structure of formaldehyde shows a central carbon atom bonded to 2 hydrogen atoms by single bonds and an oxygen atom by a double bond. The oxygen atom carries two lone pairs of electrons. Angle between the single bond between carbon and hydrogen atoms and the double bond between carbon and oxygen atoms is 122 degrees, and angle between the two single bonds between carbon and two hydrogen atoms is 116 degrees. The structure 4 is an accompanying ball-and-stick model of the molecule shows a central black sphere bonded to two white spheres by single bonds and a red sphere by a double bond.
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide42.xml]

Sample Problem 10.6: Solution (b) - Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide53.xml]
The steps are as follows: Step 1: Lewis structure of C O C l 2 shows a central carbon atom bonded to an oxygen atom by a double bond and two chlorine atoms by single bonds. The oxygen atom carries two lone pairs of electrons, and each chlorine atom carries three lone pairs of electrons. With the presence of three electron groups, the shape involves a trigonal planar arrangement. Step 2: With one double bond, the angle involved in C l single bond C double bond O is greater than 120 degrees and the angle involved in C l single bond C single bond C l is less than 120 degrees. Step 3: With the presence of three bonding groups, the following molecular shape with the general formula A X 3 is obtained. The shape shows a central carbon atom bonded to an oxygen atom by a double bond and two chlorine atoms by single bonds. The oxygen atom carries two lone pairs of electrons, and each chlorine atom carries three lone pairs of electrons. Angle between the double bond and one of the single bonds is labeled as 124.5 degrees, and the angle between the two single bonds is labeled as 111 degrees.

Molecular shape of carbon dioxide shows a central carbon atom bonded to two oxygen atoms by double bonds. Each oxygen atom carries two lone pairs of electrons. Two polar arrows from the central carbon atom point toward the oxygen atoms. Electron density model of carbon dioxide shows a central black sphere bonded to two red spheres. Each sphere is surrounded by spherical-shaped electron cloud. The electron cloud around the black sphere is depicted in blue color, and the electron cloud around the red sphere is depicted in red color.

Molecular shape of water molecule shows a central oxygen atom bonded to two hydrogen atoms by single bonds. The oxygen atom carries two lone pairs of electrons. Two polar arrows from the two hydrogen atoms point toward the oxygen atom. A central polar arrow points through the center of the oxygen atom along the lone pairs. Electron density model of water molecule shows a central red sphere bonded to two white spheres. Each sphere is surrounded by spherical-shaped electron cloud. The electron cloud around the red sphere is depicted in red color, and the electron cloud around the red white sphere is depicted in blue color.

Ball-and-stick model of sucrose shows a five-member ring bonded to a six-member ring through an oxygen atom. The five member ring shows four carbon atoms and an oxygen atom. The first and fourth carbon atoms are further bonded to a hydroxymethyl group each. The second and third carbon atoms are further bonded to a hydroxyl group each. The first carbon atom is involved in the oxygen linkage. The six member ring shows five carbon atoms and an oxygen atom. The first carbon atom is involved in the oxygen linkage. The second, third, and fourth carbon atoms are further bonded to a hydroxyl group each. The fifth carbon atom is further bonded to a hydroxyl methyl group. Ball-and-stick model of sucralose shows hydroxyl group bonded to the third carbon atom of the five-member ring, hydroxyl group bonded to the second carbon atom and hydroxyl group bonded to the hydroxymethyl group of the six-member ring replaced by chlorine atom.
